title: Two Sum
description: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target. You may assume that each input would have exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice. You can return the answer in any order.
hints: Think about what complement you need for each number to reach the target.
Can you use a hash map to store numbers you've already seen and look up complements in O(1)?
For each number x, check if (target - x) exists in your hash map. If yes, return both indices. Otherwise, store x and its index.

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
description: You are given an array prices where prices[i] is the price of a given stock on the ith day. You want to maximize your profit by choosing a single day to buy one stock and choosing a different day in the future to sell that stock. Return the maximum profit you can achieve from this transaction. If you cannot achieve any profit, return 0.

examples.explanation: Buy on day 2 (price=1) and sell on day 5 (price=6), profit = 6-1 = 5.
No profit is possible since prices only decrease.

hints: You need to buy before you sell. Track the minimum price seen so far.
For each day, what is the maximum profit if you sell today? It is today's price minus the minimum price seen before today.
Use a single pass: maintain min_price and max_profit. For each price, update max_profit = max(max_profit, price - min_price), then update min_price = min(min_price, price).
solution:
def maxProfit(prices):
    min_price = float('inf')
    max_profit = 0
    for price in prices:
        max_profit = max(max_profit, price - min_price)
        min_price = min(min_price, price)
    return max_profit

title: Maximum Subarray
description: Given an integer array nums, find the subarray with the largest sum and return its sum. A subarray is a contiguous non-empty sequence of elements within an array. This is a classic problem that can be solved optimally using Kadane's algorithm.

examples.explanation: The subarray [4,-1,2,1] has the largest sum = 6.
The subarray [5,4,-1,7,8] has the largest sum = 23.

hints: Think about when it makes sense to start a new subarray vs extending the current one.
If the current running sum becomes negative, should you continue or reset? Resetting to 0 (or the current element) is better.
Kadane's algorithm: current_sum = max(num, current_sum + num). This decides to either start fresh at num or extend. Track global max_sum throughout.
solution:
def maxSubArray(nums):
    max_sum = nums[0]
    current_sum = nums[0]
    for num in nums[1:]:
        # Either start new subarray at num, or extend current
        current_sum = max(num, current_sum + num)
        max_sum = max(max_sum, current_sum)
    return max_sum

title: 3Sum
description: Given an integer array nums, return all the triplets [nums[i], nums[j], nums[k]] such that i != j, i != k, j != k, and nums[i] + nums[j] + nums[k] == 0. The solution set must not contain duplicate triplets. Order of output triplets does not matter.
hints: Sort the array first. This allows you to use two pointers and skip duplicates easily.
Fix one element nums[i] and use two pointers (left=i+1, right=n-1) to find pairs that sum to -nums[i].
After sorting: for each i, skip duplicates. Then use two pointers: if sum < 0 move left++, if sum > 0 move right--, if sum == 0 record and skip duplicates on both sides.

title: Trapping Rain Water
description: Given n non-negative integers representing an elevation map where the width of each bar is 1, compute how much water it can trap after raining. Water is trapped between bars based on the minimum of the maximum heights to the left and right of each position.

examples.explanation: 6 units of rain water are trapped.
9 units of rain water are trapped.

hints: Water trapped at position i = min(max_left, max_right) - height[i]. Think about how to compute max_left and max_right efficiently.
You can precompute left_max and right_max arrays in O(n), then calculate water at each position.
Two-pointer approach: maintain left_max and right_max pointers. If left_max < right_max, process left side (water = left_max - height[left]); otherwise process right side. This achieves O(1) space.

title: Valid Anagram
description: Given two strings s and t, return true if t is an anagram of s, and false otherwise. An anagram is a word or phrase formed by rearranging the letters of a different word or phrase, using all the original letters exactly once.
constraints: s and t consist of lowercase English letters

examples.explanation: Both strings contain the same characters with the same frequencies.

hints: Two strings are anagrams if they have the same character frequencies. What data structure tracks frequencies?
Use a Counter (frequency map) for string s, then decrement for string t. If all counts reach zero, it's an anagram.
Simple approach: Counter(s) == Counter(t). Or sort both strings and compare. The Counter approach is O(n) time.

title: Valid Palindrome
description: A phrase is a palindrome if, after converting all uppercase letters to lowercase and removing all non-alphanumeric characters, it reads the same forward and backward. Given a string s, return true if it is a palindrome, or false otherwise.
constraints: s consists only of printable ASCII characters

examples.input: s = 'A man, a plan, a canal: Panama'
examples.explanation: After filtering: 'amanaplanacanalpanama' which is a palindrome.
After filtering: 'raceacar' which is not a palindrome.

hints: First clean the string by removing non-alphanumeric characters and converting to lowercase.
After cleaning, use two pointers from both ends moving toward the center.
Use left and right pointers. Skip non-alphanumeric chars. Compare s[left] and s[right]. If they differ, return False. If left >= right, return True.

title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
description: Given a string s, find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters. A substring is a contiguous sequence of characters within the string. You need to find the maximum length such substring.
constraints: s consists of English letters, digits, symbols and spaces
hints: This is a sliding window problem. Maintain a window [left, right] with no repeating characters.
Use a set or map to track characters in the current window. Expand right; when duplicate found, shrink from left.
Use a dict mapping char to its latest index. When s[right] is already in window, move left to max(left, last_seen[s[right]] + 1). Update max length each step.

title: Group Anagrams
description: Given an array of strings strs, group the anagrams together. You can return the answer in any order. An anagram is formed by rearranging all letters of another word using each original letter exactly once.
constraints: strs[i] consists of lowercase English letters
hints: Two strings are anagrams if their sorted versions are equal. Use the sorted string as a key.
Build a dictionary where keys are sorted strings and values are lists of anagram strings.
from collections import defaultdict: use defaultdict(list). For each word, key = tuple(sorted(word)). Append word to groups[key]. Return list of groups.values().

title: Minimum Window Substring
description: Given two strings s and t of lengths m and n respectively, return the minimum window substring of s such that every character in t (including duplicates) is included in the window. If there is no such substring, return the empty string. The answer is guaranteed to be unique.
constraints: s and t consist of uppercase and lowercase English letters

examples.explanation: The minimum window substring 'BANC' includes 'A', 'B', and 'C' from string t.
Both 'a's from t must be included in the window. No window covers 'aa'.

hints: Use a sliding window. Expand the right pointer to include all chars of t. Then shrink from the left while the window is still valid.
Track character frequencies with two counters: one for t, one for the current window. Track how many distinct chars from t are satisfied.
Use 'formed' counter for how many t-chars are fully satisfied. When formed == required, try shrinking. Track the minimum window size and its position throughout.

title: Reverse Linked List
description: Given the head of a singly linked list, reverse the list, and return the reversed list. You should reverse the pointers of each node to point to the previous node instead of the next node.
constraints: The number of nodes in the list is in the range [0, 5000]
hints: You need to change each node's next pointer to point to its previous node. Use three pointers: prev, current, and next.
Iterate through: save next node, set current.next = prev, move prev to current, move current to saved next.
prev=None, curr=head. Loop: next_node=curr.next, curr.next=prev, prev=curr, curr=next_node. Return prev (new head).
solution:
def reverseList(head):
    prev = None
    curr = head
    while curr:
        next_node = curr.next  # save next
        curr.next = prev       # reverse pointer
        prev = curr            # advance prev
        curr = next_node       # advance curr
    return prev  # new head

title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
description: You are given the heads of two sorted linked lists list1 and list2. Merge the two lists into one sorted list. The list should be made by splicing together the nodes of the first two lists. Return the head of the merged linked list.
constraints: The number of nodes in both lists is in the range [0, 50]
Both list1 and list2 are sorted in non-decreasing order

examples.explanation: Nodes are merged in sorted order.
Both empty lists merge to empty.

hints: Use a dummy head node to simplify edge cases. Maintain a current pointer for building the result list.
At each step, compare the current nodes of both lists and append the smaller one to the result. Advance that list's pointer.
Create dummy node. current = dummy. While both lists exist: append smaller node, advance that pointer. Append remaining list. Return dummy.next.

title: Linked List Cycle
description: Given head, the head of a linked list, determine if the linked list has a cycle in it. A cycle exists if some node in the list can be reached again by continuously following the next pointer. Return true if there is a cycle, false otherwise.
constraints: The number of nodes in the list is in the range [0, 10^4]

examples.explanation: There is a cycle with tail connecting to the node at index 1.

hints: Can you detect a cycle without extra memory? Think about two pointers moving at different speeds.
Floyd's Cycle Detection: use a slow pointer (1 step) and a fast pointer (2 steps). If they meet, there's a cycle.
slow = fast = head. Loop: slow=slow.next, fast=fast.next.next. If fast is None or fast.next is None, return False. If slow==fast, return True.

title: Remove Nth Node From End of List
description: Given the head of a linked list, remove the nth node from the end of the list and return its head. You must complete this in one pass through the list.
constraints: The number of nodes in the list is sz

examples.explanation: The 2nd node from the end (value 4) is removed.

hints: Use two pointers separated by n nodes. When the fast pointer reaches the end, the slow pointer will be at the node just before the target.
Advance the fast pointer n+1 steps ahead. Then move both pointers until fast reaches the end. slow.next is the node to remove.
Use a dummy node before head. fast=dummy, slow=dummy. Advance fast n+1 times. Then advance both until fast is None. slow.next = slow.next.next.
solution:
def removeNthFromEnd(head, n):
    dummy = ListNode(0, head)
    fast = slow = dummy
    # Move fast n+1 steps ahead
    for _ in range(n + 1):
        fast = fast.next
    # Move both until fast is None
    while fast:
        fast = fast.next
        slow = slow.next
    # Remove the target node
    slow.next = slow.next.next
    return dummy.next

title: Reorder List
description: You are given the head of a singly linked-list L0->L1->...->Ln-1->Ln. Reorder it to: L0->Ln->L1->Ln-1->L2->Ln-2->... You may not modify the values in the list's nodes. Only nodes themselves may be changed.
constraints: The number of nodes in the list is in the range [1, 5 * 10^4]

examples.explanation: Interleave first half with reversed second half.

hints: Break this into 3 steps: 1) Find the middle of the list. 2) Reverse the second half. 3) Merge the two halves alternately.
Use slow/fast pointers to find the middle. Then reverse the second half in-place. Then merge.
After finding mid: reverse from mid.next. Then merge first half and reversed second half alternately by updating next pointers.
solution:
def reorderList(head):
    # Step 1: Find middle
    slow, fast = head, head
    while fast.next and fast.next.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next
    # Step 2: Reverse second half
    prev, curr = None, slow.next
    slow.next = None  # split list
    while curr:
        nxt = curr.next
        curr.next = prev
        prev = curr
        curr = nxt
    # Step 3: Merge two halves
    first, second = head, prev
    while second:
        tmp1, tmp2 = first.next, second.next
        first.next = second
        second.next = tmp1
        first = tmp1
        second = tmp2

title: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
description: Given the root of a binary tree, return its maximum depth. The maximum depth is the number of nodes along the longest path from the root node down to the farthest leaf node.
constraints: The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [0, 10^4]

examples.explanation: The longest path is 3->20->15 or 3->20->7, which has 3 nodes.

hints: Think recursively: the depth of a tree = 1 + max(depth of left subtree, depth of right subtree).
Base case: if node is None, depth is 0.
def maxDepth(root): if not root: return 0; return 1 + max(maxDepth(root.left), maxDepth(root.right))

title: Invert Binary Tree
description: Given the root of a binary tree, invert the tree (mirror it), and return its root. Inverting means swapping the left and right children of every node in the tree.
constraints: The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [0, 100]

examples.explanation: Left and right subtrees are swapped at every level.

hints: Think recursively: to invert a tree, swap the left and right children, then recursively invert each subtree.
Base case: if node is None, return None.
def invertTree(root): if not root: return None; root.left, root.right = invertTree(root.right), invertTree(root.left); return root

title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
description: Given the root of a binary tree, return the level order traversal of its nodes' values (i.e., from left to right, level by level). Return a list of lists where each inner list contains values at that level.
constraints: The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [0, 2000]
hints: Level order traversal is BFS. Use a queue to process nodes level by level.
Process all nodes at the current level before moving to the next. Track the size of the queue at the start of each level.
Use deque. While queue: level_size=len(queue). For each in range(level_size): pop left, append children, collect values. Append level to result.

title: Validate Binary Search Tree
description: Given the root of a binary tree, determine if it is a valid binary search tree (BST). A valid BST means every node's left subtree contains only nodes with keys less than the node's key, every right subtree contains only nodes greater, and both subtrees must also be valid BSTs.
constraints: The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [1, 10^4]

examples.explanation: The right child has value 4 which is less than root 5.

hints: Don't just check if left.val < root.val and right.val > root.val — you need to check all descendants, not just direct children.
Pass down min and max bounds for each subtree. For the right subtree of node x, all values must be > x.val.
def validate(node, min_val, max_val): if not node: return True; if not (min_val < node.val < max_val): return False; return validate(left, min_val, node.val) and validate(right, node.val, max_val)

title: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
description: Design an algorithm to serialize and deserialize a binary tree. Serialization converts a tree to a string; deserialization reconstructs the tree from that string. There is no restriction on how your serialization/deserialization algorithm should work.
constraints: The number of nodes in the tree is in the range [0, 10^4]

examples.explanation: Tree serialized and deserialized back to original.

hints: Use BFS or DFS traversal for serialization. Use 'null' to represent missing nodes.
For DFS preorder: serialize as 'val,left,right'. Deserialize by reading values one at a time using an index/iterator.
serialize: preorder DFS, write val or 'N' for null. deserialize: use iterator over split string, read val, if 'N' return None, else create node and recurse.

title: Number of Islands
description: Given an m x n 2D binary grid where '1' represents land and '0' represents water, return the number of islands. An island is surrounded by water and is formed by connecting adjacent lands horizontally or vertically.

examples.explanation: All connected land forms one island.

hints: Iterate through the grid. When you find a '1', you've found an island. Now you need to mark all connected land as visited.
Use DFS or BFS starting from each unvisited '1' to mark the entire island (change '1' to '0' or use a visited set).
count=0. For each cell that is '1': count++, then DFS to mark all connected '1's as '0'. Return count.

title: Clone Graph
description: Given a reference of a node in a connected undirected graph, return a deep copy (clone) of the graph. Each node contains a value and a list of its neighbors. The graph is represented as an adjacency list.
constraints: The number of nodes in the graph is in the range [0, 100]
No repeated edges and no self-loops

examples.explanation: Deep copy of the graph with the same structure.

hints: Use a hash map to map original nodes to their cloned counterparts. This prevents infinite loops for cyclic graphs.
DFS or BFS traversal: when you visit a node, create its clone immediately and add to the map. Then recursively clone all neighbors.
visited = {}. dfs(node): if node in visited return visited[node]. clone = Node(node.val). visited[node] = clone. For each neighbor: clone.neighbors.append(dfs(neighbor)). Return clone.

title: Course Schedule
description: There are numCourses courses labeled 0 to numCourses-1. Given an array prerequisites where prerequisites[i] = [ai, bi] indicates you must take course bi first before taking course ai, return true if you can finish all courses. This is essentially a cycle detection problem in a directed graph.
hints: This is a cycle detection problem. If a directed graph has a cycle, you can't finish all courses.
Use DFS with 3 states for each node: 0 (unvisited), 1 (visiting/in current path), 2 (visited/done). If you encounter state 1 during DFS, there's a cycle.
Build adjacency list. DFS each node: if state[node]==1 return False (cycle). If state[node]==2 return True (already done). Set state=1, recurse on neighbors, set state=2.

title: Pacific Atlantic Water Flow
description: Given an m x n rectangular island that borders both the Pacific and Atlantic oceans. Rain water can flow to neighboring cells (up/down/left/right) if the height is equal or lower. Find all cells where water can flow to both the Pacific and Atlantic ocean.

examples.explanation: These cells can flow to both oceans.

hints: Instead of flowing from each cell outward, reverse the flow: start from ocean borders and find all cells reachable going uphill.
Do two BFS/DFS from Pacific borders (top row, left col) and Atlantic borders (bottom row, right col). Cells in both sets are the answer.
BFS from all Pacific border cells (allow flow if neighbor height >= current). Same for Atlantic. Intersection is the answer.

title: Word Ladder
description: A transformation sequence from word beginWord to word endWord using a dictionary wordList is valid if each transformed word differs by exactly one letter, each intermediate word must exist in wordList, and beginWord does not need to be in wordList. Return the number of words in the shortest transformation sequence, or 0 if no such sequence exists.
constraints: All words consist of lowercase English letters
hints: This is a shortest path problem in an unweighted graph. BFS gives the shortest path.
Each word is a node. Two words are connected if they differ by exactly one character. BFS from beginWord to endWord.
Use BFS with a queue of (word, length). For each word, try changing each character to a-z. If the new word is in the word set (and not visited), add to queue. Return length when endWord is found.

title: Climbing Stairs
description: You are climbing a staircase. It takes n steps to reach the top. Each time you can either climb 1 or 2 steps. In how many distinct ways can you climb to the top? This is equivalent to computing the nth Fibonacci number.
hints: Think about the last step: you either came from step n-1 (took 1 step) or step n-2 (took 2 steps).
ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2). This is the Fibonacci recurrence.
Use DP with two variables: prev1 (ways to reach n-1) and prev2 (ways to reach n-2). Iterate and update.

title: Coin Change
description: You are given an integer array coins representing coins of different denominations and an integer amount representing a total amount of money. Return the fewest number of coins needed to make up that amount. If that amount cannot be made up by any combination of the coins, return -1. You may use an infinite number of each coin.
hints: This is an unbounded knapsack / BFS problem. Think bottom-up: dp[i] = minimum coins to make amount i.
dp[0] = 0. For each amount from 1 to amount: dp[i] = min(dp[i - coin] + 1) for all valid coins.
Initialize dp array of size (amount+1) with infinity. dp[0]=0. For each i from 1 to amount: for each coin <= i: dp[i] = min(dp[i], dp[i-coin]+1). Return dp[amount] if not infinity, else -1.

title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
description: Given an integer array nums, return the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence. A subsequence is derived from the array by deleting some elements without changing the order of remaining elements.

examples.explanation: The longest increasing subsequence is [2,3,7,101], length 4.

hints: dp[i] = length of the longest increasing subsequence ending at index i. Base case: dp[i] = 1 for all i.
For each i, look at all j < i where nums[j] < nums[i]. dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1).
O(n^2) DP: dp[i] = 1 + max(dp[j] for j<i if nums[j]<nums[i]). O(n log n) is possible with binary search + patience sorting.

title: Edit Distance
description: Given two strings word1 and word2, return the minimum number of operations required to convert word1 to word2. The allowed operations are: Insert a character, Delete a character, Replace a character. This is the classic Levenshtein distance problem.
constraints: word1 and word2 consist of lowercase English letters
hints: dp[i][j] = minimum operations to convert word1[0..i-1] to word2[0..j-1]. Build a 2D table.
If word1[i-1] == word2[j-1]: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] (no operation needed). Else: dp[i][j] = 1 + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]) for delete/insert/replace.
Initialize: dp[0][j] = j (insert j chars), dp[i][0] = i (delete i chars). Fill row by row. Return dp[m][n].

title: Burst Balloons
description: You are given n balloons, indexed from 0 to n-1. Each balloon is painted with a number on it represented by nums. You are asked to burst all the balloons. If you burst balloon i you will get nums[i-1]*nums[i]*nums[i+1] coins. After bursting, the neighbors change. Return the maximum coins you can collect.
hints: Think about which balloon you burst LAST in a range, not first. The last balloon in range (l,r) still has its original neighbors l and r.
dp[l][r] = max coins from bursting all balloons between l and r (exclusive, with boundary balloons l and r staying). Try each k as the last balloon burst.
Add sentinel 1s at both ends. dp[l][r] = max(nums[l]*nums[k]*nums[r] + dp[l][k] + dp[k][r]) for k in (l+1, r). Build bottom-up by window size.

title: Subsets
description: Given an integer array nums of unique elements, return all possible subsets (the power set). The solution set must not contain duplicate subsets. Return the solution in any order.
constraints: All the numbers of nums are unique
hints: Use backtracking: at each step, decide to include or exclude the current element. Collect the current state at each node of the recursion tree.
Backtrack with a start index. At each call, add the current path to results. Then try adding each element from start to n-1.
def backtrack(start, path): result.append(path[:]). For i in range(start, n): path.append(nums[i]), backtrack(i+1, path), path.pop()

title: Permutations
description: Given an array nums of distinct integers, return all the possible permutations. A permutation is an arrangement of all members of a set. You can return the answer in any order.
constraints: All the integers of nums are unique
hints: At each step, pick one number from the remaining unused numbers. Continue until all numbers are used.
Use a visited/used set to track which numbers are already in the current permutation.
def backtrack(path): if len(path)==n: result.append(path[:]); return. For each num in nums not in used: used.add(num), path.append(num), backtrack(path), path.pop(), used.remove(num)

title: Combination Sum
description: Given an array of distinct integers candidates and a target integer target, return a list of all unique combinations of candidates where the chosen numbers sum to target. The same number may be chosen from candidates an unlimited number of times. Two combinations are unique if the frequency of at least one of the chosen numbers is different.
constraints: All elements of candidates are distinct
hints: Use backtracking. At each step, choose to include a candidate (and stay at the same index since reuse is allowed) or move on.
Sort candidates first to enable early termination. If remaining target < 0, backtrack.
def backtrack(start, path, remaining): if remaining==0 add path to results. if remaining<0 return. For i in range(start,n): backtrack(i, path+[candidates[i]], remaining-candidates[i])

title: N-Queens
description: The n-queens puzzle is the problem of placing n queens on an n x n chessboard such that no two queens attack each other. Given an integer n, return all distinct solutions to the n-queens puzzle. Each solution contains a distinct board configuration where 'Q' and '.' indicate a queen and an empty space respectively.

examples.explanation: Two distinct solutions to the 4-queens problem.

hints: Place queens row by row. For each row, try each column. A column is valid if no other queen is in the same column, or on the same diagonal.
Track used columns, positive diagonals (row-col), and negative diagonals (row+col) to quickly check validity.
For each row, try each col not in cols/pos_diag/neg_diag. Place queen, recurse to next row. If row==n, add board to results.
solution:
def solveNQueens(n):
    cols = set()
    pos_diag = set()  # r - c
    neg_diag = set()  # r + c
    board = [['.']*n for _ in range(n)]
    result = []
    def backtrack(row):
        if row == n:
            result.append([''.join(r) for r in board])
            return
        for col in range(n):
            if col in cols or (row-col) in pos_diag or (row+col) in neg_diag:
                continue
            cols.add(col); pos_diag.add(row-col); neg_diag.add(row+col)
            board[row][col] = 'Q'
            backtrack(row + 1)
            cols.remove(col); pos_diag.remove(row-col); neg_diag.remove(row+col)
            board[row][col] = '.'
    backtrack(0)
    return result

title: Sudoku Solver
description: Write a program to solve a Sudoku puzzle by filling in the empty cells. A sudoku solution must satisfy: each of the digits 1-9 must occur exactly once in each row, column, and 3x3 sub-box. Empty cells are indicated by '.'.
constraints: It is guaranteed that the input board has only one solution

examples.explanation: Each row, column, and 3x3 box contains 1-9 exactly once.

hints: Find the first empty cell. Try placing digits 1-9. If valid (not in row/col/box), place and recurse. If recursion succeeds, done. Else backtrack.
Use three sets of sets: rows[i], cols[j], boxes[i//3][j//3] to track used digits for O(1) validity checks.
is_valid(r,c,num): return num not in rows[r] and num not in cols[c] and num not in boxes[r//3][c//3]. Backtrack places num if valid, recurses, and removes if needed.

title: Kth Largest Element in Array
description: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the kth largest element in the array. Note that it is the kth largest element in sorted order, not the kth distinct element. Can you solve it without sorting?
hints: One approach: use a min-heap of size k. The top of the heap is always the kth largest.
Add each element. If heap size > k, remove the minimum. At the end, the top of the heap is the kth largest.
import heapq. heap = []. For num in nums: heapq.heappush(heap, num). if len(heap) > k: heapq.heappop(heap). return heap[0]

title: Top K Frequent Elements
description: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the k most frequent elements. You may return the answer in any order. Your algorithm's time complexity must be better than O(n log n).
constraints: k is in the range [1, the number of unique elements in the array]
hints: Count frequencies with a Counter, then find the k elements with the highest counts.
Use a min-heap of size k storing (frequency, element). Or use bucket sort.
Bucket sort approach: buckets[freq] = [elements]. Count frequencies. Create n+1 buckets. Fill buckets by freq. Traverse from high to low freq collecting k elements.

title: Task Scheduler
description: Given a characters array tasks representing CPU tasks, each labeled with an uppercase letter, and an integer n representing the cooldown period between the same tasks, return the minimum number of intervals the CPU takes to finish all the given tasks. During the cooldown, the CPU can be idle or execute a different task.

examples.explanation: No cooldown, just execute all 6 tasks.

hints: Think greedily: always execute the most frequent remaining task first. Use a max-heap for this.
After executing a task, it must wait n cycles before it can run again. Use a queue to track (count, available_time) for cooling tasks.
Use max-heap + queue. Each round: if heap not empty pop most frequent, decrement count, if count>0 add to queue with available_time=time+n+1. Dequeue tasks whose cooldown has expired.
solution:
import heapq
from collections import Counter, deque
def leastInterval(tasks, n):
    count = Counter(tasks)
    max_heap = [-c for c in count.values()]
    heapq.heapify(max_heap)
    queue = deque()  # (-count, available_time)
    time = 0
    while max_heap or queue:
        time += 1
        if max_heap:
            cnt = 1 + heapq.heappop(max_heap)  # decrement
            if cnt < 0:
                queue.append((cnt, time + n))
        if queue and queue[0][1] == time:
            heapq.heappush(max_heap, queue.popleft()[0])
    return time

title: Merge K Sorted Lists
description: You are given an array of k linked-lists lists, each linked-list is sorted in ascending order. Merge all the linked-lists into one sorted linked-list and return it. The resulting list should be sorted in ascending order.
constraints: lists[i] is sorted in ascending order
The sum of all lists[i].length won't exceed 10^4

examples.explanation: All three lists merged and sorted.

hints: Extend Merge Two Sorted Lists to k lists. A min-heap lets you always pick the globally smallest current node.
Push the head of each non-null list into a min-heap. Pop the minimum, append to result, push its next node if it exists.
Use heapq with (node.val, index, node) tuples (index breaks ties). Pop smallest, append to result list, push next node. Return dummy.next.

title: Find Median from Data Stream
description: The MedianFinder class supports two operations: addNum(int num) adds an integer from the data stream to the data structure, and findMedian() returns the median of current data elements. If the number of elements is even, the median is the mean of the two middle elements.
constraints: There will be at least one element before calling findMedian
At most 5 * 10^4 calls will be made to addNum and findMedian

examples.explanation: After adding 1,2 median is 1.5. After adding 3 median is 2.0.

hints: Use two heaps: a max-heap for the lower half, a min-heap for the upper half. Keep them balanced.
The median is either the top of the max-heap (if sizes differ) or the average of both tops (if equal).
addNum: push to max_heap (negate). If max_heap.top > min_heap.top, rebalance. Ensure len(max_heap) >= len(min_heap). findMedian: if equal return avg of tops, else return max_heap.top.

title: Binary Search
description: Given an array of integers nums which is sorted in ascending order, and an integer target, write a function to search target in nums. If target exists, return its index. Otherwise, return -1. You must write an algorithm with O(log n) runtime complexity.
constraints: All the integers in nums are unique
nums is sorted in ascending order
hints: Use left and right pointers. Compare the middle element to target.
If nums[mid] == target, return mid. If nums[mid] < target, search right half (left = mid+1). Else search left half (right = mid-1).
left=0, right=len(nums)-1. While left<=right: mid=(left+right)//2. Check nums[mid] vs target. Return -1 if not found.

title: First Bad Version
description: You are a product manager and currently leading a team to develop a new product. Unfortunately, the latest version of your product fails the quality check. All versions after a bad version are also bad. Given n versions [1, 2, ..., n] and an API isBadVersion(version), find the first bad version.

examples.explanation: Calling isBadVersion(3) returns false. Calling isBadVersion(5) returns true. Calling isBadVersion(4) returns true. So 4 is the first bad version.

hints: Binary search on version numbers 1 to n. The answer is the leftmost bad version.
If mid is bad, the answer is mid or earlier (right = mid). If mid is good, answer is after mid (left = mid + 1).
left=1, right=n. While left<right: mid=(left+right)//2. if isBadVersion(mid): right=mid. else: left=mid+1. Return left.

title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
description: There is an integer array nums sorted in ascending order with distinct values that has been rotated at an unknown pivot index k. Given the array nums after the rotation and an integer target, return the index of target if it is in nums, or -1 if it is not in nums. You must write an algorithm with O(log n) runtime complexity.
constraints: nums is an ascending array rotated at some pivot
hints: Even though the array is rotated, one half of [left, mid] or [mid, right] is always sorted. Use this to determine which half to search.
If nums[left] <= nums[mid], the left half is sorted. Check if target is in this sorted range. Otherwise search the right half.
At each step: if left half sorted (nums[left]<=nums[mid]): if nums[left]<=target<nums[mid]: right=mid-1. else: left=mid+1. Else right half is sorted: similar logic.

title: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
description: Suppose an array of length n sorted in ascending order is rotated between 1 and n times. Given the sorted rotated array nums of unique elements, return the minimum element of this array. You must write an algorithm that runs in O(log n) time.
constraints: All the integers of nums are unique
nums is sorted and rotated between 1 and n times

examples.explanation: The original array was [1,2,3,4,5] rotated 3 times.

hints: The minimum element is the inflection point of the rotation. Use binary search to find it.
If nums[mid] > nums[right], the minimum is in the right half. Otherwise it's in the left half (including mid).
left=0, right=n-1. While left<right: mid=(left+right)//2. if nums[mid]>nums[right]: left=mid+1. else: right=mid. return nums[left].

title: Median of Two Sorted Arrays
description: Given two sorted arrays nums1 and nums2 of size m and n respectively, return the median of the two sorted arrays. The overall run time complexity should be O(log (m+n)).
hints: Binary search on the partition point in the smaller array. The partition divides both arrays into left and right halves of equal total size.
A valid partition satisfies: max(left1, left2) <= min(right1, right2). Binary search to find this partition.
Binary search on nums1 (smaller). Partition at i in nums1 and j=(m+n+1)//2-i in nums2. Check max_left <= min_right. Adjust binary search accordingly.

title: Maximum Average Subarray I
description: You are given an integer array nums consisting of n elements, and an integer k. Find a contiguous subarray whose length is equal to k that has the maximum average value and return this value. Any answer with a calculation error less than 10^-5 will be accepted.
hints: Use a fixed-size sliding window of size k. Compute the sum of the first k elements, then slide the window.
When sliding: add the new element (right) and subtract the element leaving the window (left = right - k).
Initialize window_sum = sum(nums[:k]). max_sum = window_sum. For i in range(k, n): window_sum += nums[i] - nums[i-k]. max_sum = max(max_sum, window_sum). return max_sum/k.
solution:
def findMaxAverage(nums, k):
    window_sum = sum(nums[:k])
    max_sum = window_sum
    for i in range(k, len(nums)):
        window_sum += nums[i] - nums[i - k]
        max_sum = max(max_sum, window_sum)
    return max_sum / k

title: Longest Repeating Character Replacement
description: You are given a string s and an integer k. You can choose any character of the string and change it to any other uppercase English character. You can perform this operation at most k times. Return the length of the longest substring containing the same letter you can get after performing the above operations.
constraints: s consists of only uppercase English letters

examples.explanation: Replace the two A's or two B's with the other, resulting in 'AAAA' or 'BBBB'.
Replace one 'A' in the third position to get 'AABBBBA' or similar.

hints: Use a sliding window. The window is valid if (window_size - max_freq_in_window) <= k. We can replace the minority characters.
Maintain a frequency count of characters in the window. Track the maximum frequency seen so far.
Expand right. If (right-left+1) - max_freq > k: shrink left, update count. Update max_freq. Track max window size.
solution:
def characterReplacement(s, k):
    count = {}
    max_freq = 0
    left = 0
    result = 0
    for right in range(len(s)):
        count[s[right]] = count.get(s[right], 0) + 1
        max_freq = max(max_freq, count[s[right]])
        # Shrink if more than k replacements needed
        while (right - left + 1) - max_freq > k:
            count[s[left]] -= 1
            left += 1
        result = max(result, right - left + 1)
    return result

title: Permutation in String
description: Given two strings s1 and s2, return true if s2 contains a permutation of s1, or false otherwise. In other words, return true if one of s1's permutations is a substring of s2. A permutation is an arrangement of characters.
constraints: s1 and s2 consist of lowercase English letters

examples.explanation: s2 contains one permutation of s1 ('ba').
No permutation of s1 is a substring of s2.

hints: A permutation has the same character frequencies. Use a fixed sliding window of size len(s1) and compare frequency maps.
Build the frequency map for s1. Slide a window of size len(s1) across s2. If frequencies match, return True.
Use two Counter objects (or arrays of 26). Add char entering window, remove char leaving. Compare counts. O(26) comparison.
solution:
from collections import Counter
def checkInclusion(s1, s2):
    if len(s1) > len(s2): return False
    need = Counter(s1)
    window = Counter(s2[:len(s1)])
    if window == need: return True
    for i in range(len(s1), len(s2)):
        window[s2[i]] += 1
        old = s2[i - len(s1)]
        window[old] -= 1
        if window[old] == 0:
            del window[old]
        if window == need:
            return True
    return False

title: Minimum Size Subarray Sum
description: Given an array of positive integers nums and a positive integer target, return the minimal length of a subarray whose sum is greater than or equal to target. If there is no such subarray, return 0 instead.

examples.explanation: Subarray [4,3] has minimal length 2 with sum >= 7.

hints: Use a variable-size sliding window. Expand the right pointer to increase sum. When sum >= target, shrink from the left.
Track the minimum window size whenever sum >= target. Keep shrinking the left until sum < target.

left=0, window_sum=0, min_len=inf. For right in range(n): window_sum+=nums[right]. While window_sum>=target: min_len=min(min_len, right-left+1); window_sum-=nums[left]; left+=1. Return min_len if finite else 0.
solution:
def minSubArrayLen(target, nums):
    left = 0
    window_sum = 0
    min_len = float('inf')
    for right in range(len(nums)):
        window_sum += nums[right]
        while window_sum >= target:
            min_len = min(min_len, right - left + 1)
            window_sum -= nums[left]
            left += 1
    return min_len if min_len != float('inf') else 0

title: Sliding Window Maximum
description: You are given an array of integers nums, and there is a sliding window of size k which is moving from the very left of the array to the very right. You can only see the k numbers in the window. Each time the sliding window moves right by one position, return the max sliding window. Return an array of maximums.

examples.explanation: Max of each window of size 3.

hints: A naive approach checks k elements per window, O(nk). Can you do better with a monotonic deque?
Use a deque that stores indices in decreasing order of values. The front is always the index of the maximum for the current window.
Deque stores indices. For each i: remove indices out of window (< i-k+1). Pop from back while nums[deque.back] <= nums[i]. Append i. If i >= k-1: result.append(nums[deque.front]).
